Title: Attacking the gate 💥 chess puzzle
Transcript: Imagine you're attacking a castle. All your forces focus on raming down the gate, not coming in from the side. This is the gate with our elephants piling up on it. Not the importance of our own pawn here. It keeps the king on the aphi in the line of fire. It has to step forward before it can step sideways. We begin with a rook to remove the pond shield. The black queen steps into the breach. If we eliminate the queen immediately. the king steps forward and can escape. Instead, we move our other rook over to support our queen.😊Black is forced to take our queen, our rook captures back, and there are no more defenders at the gate.

Their legal persecution is the core subject of the message (life imprisonment, death penalty, and family members being imprisoned), making the group highly central to the video’s narrative.
itt_response.realistic_threat: Yes, but framed as a threat posed by the state/legal system rather than by LGBTQ people themselves. Evidence includes claims that the law results in life imprisonment, death penalty in some cases, and imprisonment of friends/family for not reporting LGBTQ people. This depicts a material threat to safety and freedom for LGBTQ individuals.

Instead, it presents the persecution as unjust and extreme. However, it does reference the concept of “homosexuals” as a category targeted by law, without providing stereotype-based “evidence” about LGBTQ behavior.

It also implies a stereotype of women as less likely to face consequences for sexual wrongdoing, using the legal outcome as “evidence.”

It does not show direct calls for violence or extermination, but it does endorse exclusionary/harsh treatment through the lens of gender-based injustice (i.e., advocating for incarceration/stronger punishment).

Title: #harpy #eagle #animal #wildlife #nature #creatures #animalfact #habitats #amazingfact #short #zoo
Transcript: 🎼The majestic world of the Hary eagle, This bird, among the largest and most powerful eagles, reigns over its kingdom from the tree tops with a wingspan of over2 me. It commands the skies with grace and precision.Reenowned for their strength, hay Eagleagles can snatch up prey, weighing up to half their body weight, monkeys, sloths, and other tree dwelling creatures often fall victim to their powerful talons.Yet, they are not mindless predators, Hary Eagleles are devoted parents, nurturing their young for nearly two years before they venture off on their own.Despite their awe inspiring presence, Harry Eagleagles are facing threats, deforestation and hunting have dramatically dwindled their numbers, placing them on the list of near threatened species.But hope is not lost, conservation efforts are underway, striving to protect and preserve the habitats of these magnificent birds.

Title: Is now the era of homosexuality ? Dave Allen
Transcript: I was talking to a man the other day who was leaving, decided to immigrate, decided to leave this country, and I said, why are you leaving, he said," it's homosexuality, I said what are you talking about?"He said 300 years ago, if you were homosexual, you were hanged drawn and quaed100 years ago, if you were homosexual, you were hanged 50 years ago, if you were homosexual, you were flogged and given 20 years in prison.went0 years ago, if you were homosexual, you were fined 200 pounds and sent to prison for two years, five years ago, if you were homosexual, you were a small, fine and you were pardoned.Go off for the warning. I said, what are you leaving for, He said, I'm going before they make it compulsory.
Label: Hateful

itt_response.target_salience: The video centers on homosexual people (sexual minorities) as the subject of the speaker’s explanation for why someone would leave the country. Their status is central to the message because the transcript repeatedly uses “if you were homosexual…” as the core framing device.
itt_response.realistic_threat: No clear material/real-world threat is shown to jobs, physical safety, health, or resources posed by homosexual people. Instead, the transcript describes historical legal punishments imposed on homosexual people, not threats originating from them.
itt_response.symbolic_threat: Yes. The transcript frames homosexuality as something associated with moral/legal order and state control (“hanged… drawn and quartered,” “flogged,” “prison,” “fined,” “pardoned,” and “before they make it compulsory”). This implies a symbolic threat to prevailing norms/values and suggests that the social order treats homosexuality as unacceptable, thereby positioning homosexuality as a trigger for punitive cultural/legal responses.
itt_response.anxiety_discomfort: The video likely creates discomfort by invoking escalating historical punishments and fear of future compulsion (“I’m going before they make it compulsory”). The unease is driven by the ominous, punitive language in the transcript and the emphasis on severe consequences; the editing appears to be a straightforward interview clip with overlaid captions, reinforcing the threatening narrative.
itt_response.stereotype_support: It reinforces a negative association between homosexuality and criminality/punishment by citing historical persecution as justification for fear and flight. While it does not use slurs or depict homosexual people as inherently dangerous, it treats homosexuality as a category that historically warranted extreme punishment, which can function as a stereotype of deviance requiring control.
itt_response.hostility_prescription: The transcript does not explicitly call for violence or direct harm by the viewer toward homosexual people. However, the implied conclusion—leaving the country to avoid future compulsory measures—supports exclusionary avoidance and suggests hostility through fear of state-imposed repression rather than acceptance.

Label: Offensive

itt_response.target_salience: No specific social group (e.g., race/ethnicity/nationality/religion) is depicted as a target. The video centers on a moral warning about an 'adulteress' and a 'fool' who would not obey teachers/instructors. The 'adulteress' functions as a role/behavioral category rather than an identifiable protected or social group.
itt_response.realistic_threat: No. The content does not present the 'adulteress' or 'fool' as a material threat to safety, jobs, health, resources, or territory. The only 'threat' is moral/spiritual (temptation leading to wrongdoing), not physical harm or resource competition.
itt_response.symbolic_threat: Yes, in a moral-symbolic sense. The text frames the 'adulteress' as a corrupting influence that draws people away from obedience and a 'narrow path.' Evidence: on-screen/transcript phrases like 'Fools cannot resist the adulteress' and 'I would not obey my teachers... or turn my ear to my instructors,' plus commentary about leaving the narrow path to 'miss out on his invitation from the adulteress.'
itt_response.anxiety_discomfort: Some discomfort is induced, but not fear of a group. The tone is admonitory/warning (religious moral instruction). Title: Discovering the Dark Origins of the Harpy in Greek Mythology
Transcript: Harpy is a creature with the body of a bird in the head of a woman. It was said to be a personification of storm winds and was known for its swift and deadly attacks In Greek mythology, there were two main harpies, Alo and oeped. They were daughters of the seagothhams and the ocean at E. They were often depicted as fierce creatures with sharp claws and teeth. and the ability to fly at great speeds. The harpies were notorious for their habit of stealing food. In fact, the word harppy is derived from the Greek word harppy, which means to snatch.They were said to swoop down on unsuspecting victims and carry off their food or even their children. The harpies were also known for their association with punishment. In some myths, They were sent by the gods to punish mortals who had committed crimes. They were particularly associated with the punish.

Title: Coming out doesn’t make your queer identity legitimate. #asexuality

Title: It’s Chest Day & You Get Shoulder Pain!
Transcript: I'm trying to put you in the worst smooth up.Here's the story of one of my patients who came to complaints of right shoulder snapping and pain with every dumbbell bench press. The first thing I did assessed technique, I noticed in the bottom of his bench press, he had a lot of shoulder extension, meaning his elbow was dropping really far down. Now, why is this an issue. Well, whenever you're performing a dumbbell bench press or a barbell bench press. If you drop that elbow too far down and going to excessive extension. It can lead to excessive load on the front side of the shoulder, which for him was causing his symptoms because of a lack.St it's not that this is a bad exercise, but if you drop that elbow too low, it can just place a lot of load on the front side of the shoulder. So my cue for him was to set his shoulder blades into a good position by retracting them at the start of the movement. This allowed him to perform that dumbbell bench press, but with better shoulder mechanics, less stress on the front of the shoulder and no more snapping.
Label: Normal

itt_response.stereotype_support: None. The video does not invoke or reinforce negative stereotypes about any protected or social group. It attributes pain to elbow drop/shoulder extension mechanics.
itt_response.hostility_prescription: No. The video does not endorse exclusion, hostility, or harm toward any group. It provides corrective guidance to reduce injury (better mechanics, less stress, no more snapping).

And they would they would hit me up on the download, hey, like what's the link, You know, And I thought they were joking at first. And no no no, no no, they were like, no, like what's the link. And I was like, oh, okay, well, I was like, here you go, know, I mean I didn't know what to really think of.In college, like I had some guys that they would say some things or they would do some things in the locker room or theyd look at you a little bit too long. football guy, I was like whatever, you know, just it's in the lock room, but sometimes you're like, who, like you know, I'm straight, chill out, but you know that's cool.

Title: The invention of Silly Putty
Transcript: 🎼Yo, remember, silly putty. The stuff was invented to defeat the Japanese in World War2. So 1943, we got this due, James Wright, and he's an engineer at G. E. Jimmy Boa, we's in the thick of World War2 and God damn it. those Japanese are controlling all the rubber we need. In a synthetic rubber substitute.Okay, bra, bra silicone oil and dark bow ray bow raise.😊Bowri So he mixes silicone oil and boic acid, but rubber's supposed to be rubber And James's mix wasn't a solid or liquid. It could kind of flow, but then it also bounce and stretched and randomly could transfer newspaper ink onto itself. I'll how you nuty pie who the good nu Then after the war ended, James rebranded his putty as silly putty and his real solid liquid sold 300 million units.
Label: Normal

itt_response.target_salience: The video explicitly references “the Japanese” as a group central to the explanation of why Silly Putty was invented (e.g., “invented to defeat the Japanese in World War2”). The Japanese are the primary social group named as the adversary driving the narrative, though the rest of the content focuses on chemistry/putty behavior.
itt_response.realistic_threat: Yes. The Japanese are framed as a material/resource threat: they are said to be “controlling all the rubber we need” during World War II, motivating the creation of a synthetic rubber substitute. The evidence presented is the claim that Japanese control rubber supply, leading to shortages and the need for an alternative material.
itt_response.symbolic_threat: Partially. The video frames the Japanese as an enemy to be “defeat[ed]” in World War II, which implies a threat to national security/war aims rather than cultural or religious values. However, it does not provide detailed claims about cultural/moral contamination; the symbolic element is mainly adversarial wartime framing.
itt_response.anxiety_discomfort: The video uses a combative, adversarial tone (“defeat,” “controlling,” “in the thick of World War2,” profanity) and war-themed imagery (damaged buildings, flags, soldiers/characters) to create tension. Editing and on-screen text emphasize conflict and scarcity (rubber needed), producing discomfort/fear primarily through the war narrative rather than through direct dehumanization.
itt_response.stereotype_support: The main negative implication is a wartime stereotype of the Japanese as controlling critical resources (rubber) and as the obstacle to technological/material progress. The video does not show explicit slurs or depict inherent criminality/biological inferiority, but it does attribute a strategic, controlling role to the group as justification for the invention.
itt_response.hostility_prescription: No clear endorsement of post-war exclusion or harm is present. The threat perception is situated in a historical WWII context and is used to explain a scientific/industrial motivation (synthetic rubber substitute). The video does not prescribe ongoing hostility toward Japanese people beyond the historical framing.

The transcript claims the speaker has been accused of “causing a national tampon supply shortage,” and implies trans women’s behavior contributes to shortages. The imagery/text also frames trans women as taking/using products in a way that affects availability (tampons). No direct evidence of real-world harm is shown in the frames beyond the claim.

Editing includes split-screen comparisons and on-screen captions that emphasize exclusionary judgments (e.g., “trash like you,” “better candidate”), reinforcing unease about trans women’s intentions and legitimacy.
itt_response.stereotype_support: It invokes negative stereotypes about trans women as deceptive or illegitimate (“we never see trans men… biological women who identifies men”), as entitled/entrenched in entitlement and wastefulness (designer bag “trash” framing), and as sexually/biologically inappropriate regarding menstrual products (“This thing does not go up inside you,” “Pay close attention… Day 12 of being a girl”). These are presented as justification for the speaker’s conclusions.
itt_response.hostility_prescription: The accumulated threat perception supports exclusionary and hostile attitudes. While the video does not show explicit physical violence, it implies social delegitimization and contempt (e.g., “trash like you,” “we probably most likely a better candidate,” and the insistence that trans women are not “real” women). This functions as a hostility prescription through dehumanization and calls for separation/exclusion from women’s spaces and norms.

These are presented as justification for the claims without clear corroborating evidence in the clip.

This constitutes a prescriptive stance against inclusion/acceptance of the targeted group.

Title: The Church Allowed Gay Marriage?! #shorts
Transcript: Did you know that the Roman Catholic Church used to be cool with same sex marriage. O, maybe not cool, but way back in the dark Ages, there was an order of uniting of two men with one famously document case between a Roman Army commander Saint Sergis and his deputies Saint Bachus. This was a ceremony, pracised historically in Orodox Christian tradition to unite together two people of the same sex.Normally men in a church recognise relationships.😊は。ind of similar to siblinghood, these ceremonies often included all things and typical wedding, the couple blessed at the altar together and usually a big banquet to celebrate after these two particular and men were torturerd and beheaded once their secret sexuality was exposed because you know it was meant to be a sexual thing and then they were both stripped of their sainthood by the church in 1969. but you know it's our history, however, the close friendship between St Serges and Baus was emphasized throughout history making them one of the most well-known example.The pair saints, so we've got that.😊

itt_response.hostility_prescription: No. The accumulated threat perception does not lead to endorsing exclusion or harm. The closing framing (“it’s our history,” “making them one of the most well-known examples”) is more consistent with recognition/normalization than hostility.

Title: 10 Year Olds Are DESTROYING Drunk Elephant At Sephora #skincare #sephora #drunkelephant

I thought it wasn't going to be terrible but I was sorely mistaken This thing was disgusting. There was a lip bm with a literal hair in it. Someone put the bronzing drops all over one of the moisturizers couldn't even be bothered to put them back. There's caps left off to shampoos.AndThere's products from the inky list mixed in it was absolutely disgusting The worst part is though teenagers kept on bumping into me Yes pretweenens kept on slamming into me and crowding the section and I for some reason kept on saying sorry sorry when they read into me childhood trauma anyways there were so many teens unsupervised and I was shocked because it wasn't just you know the stereotypical bray teenage girl but there were guys as well they drunk elephant section was foul Where are your parents.

They ruthlessly captured ships and raided coastal villages. establishing a reign of terror across the South China Sea and Southeast Asian waters. Qing X's past as a prostitute did not define her. Rather, it was her unparalleled skills, resilience and leadership that propelled her to the forefront of piracy during this era.

itt_response.realistic_threat: No. The video does not depict LGBTQ people as posing a material threat to safety, health, or resources. The discussion is about casting practices, privacy, and audience reactions to actors’ sexuality, not about harm or danger.
itt_response.symbolic_threat: No clear symbolic threat is advanced against LGBTQ people. Instead, the speaker argues against policing actors’ sexuality and against framing casting choices as problematic in a way that targets queer people. The only “threat” language is directed at the concept of “queerbaiting” and audience behavior, not at LGBTQ people as a group undermining values or culture.
itt_response.anxiety_discomfort: Some discomfort is present, but it is directed toward audience behavior and frustration about representation (e.g., “stop casting straight people as queer characters,” “it’s frustrating,” “people proceeded to force him to come out”). The tone is heated/angry in places (caption “STOP QUEERBAITING! 😡🏳️‍🌈”), but the emotional focus is on debate over casting and privacy rather than fear of LGBTQ people.
itt_response.stereotype_support: The video does not strongly reinforce negative stereotypes about LGBTQ people. However, it does engage with common discourse that treats sexuality as something that can be inferred from appearance/personality (“they don’t seem like a lesbian to me,” “don’t give off bisexual vibes”)—which can implicitly support the idea that sexuality is externally legible and should be policed. The speaker also rejects this by stating there are no personality traits behind sexuality and that casting/acting is a job.
itt_response.hostility_prescription: No. The video does not endorse exclusion or harm toward LGBTQ people. It argues for respecting privacy and for directing frustration toward producers/casting rather than targeting actors or policing sexuality.

Title: Homosexual animal behavior as a moral standard.
Transcript: I've heard this many times. Animal have homosexual relationships. That means it's natural therefore, it's okay. But where do we get the idea that typical animal behavior is okay for humans, Like, where does this come from, Like animals eat their babies, Anims steal things all the time. They just constantly steal things. And murder. I mean, no, like animal behavior is not the standard for me. This is not a Christian worldview. We're not like the animals. Man was made in the image of God. And we have a higher calling, and we have a different responsibility in creation compared to animals.
Label: Hateful

itt_response.target_salience: The video discusses homosexual relationships as a social/sexual orientation group (LGBTQ+/gay people) in the context of whether such behavior is “natural” and morally acceptable for humans. This group is central to the argument because the host directly addresses the claim that animal homosexual behavior justifies human acceptance.
itt_response.realistic_threat: No. The video does not present homosexual people as a material threat to safety, jobs, health, or resources. The only “evidence” offered is moral/behavioral comparison to animals, not any concrete harm caused by the group.
itt_response.symbolic_threat: Yes, indirectly. The video frames the pro-acceptance argument (“it’s natural therefore it’s okay”) as coming from a flawed worldview and contrasts it with a Christian moral framework. It implies that adopting animal behavior as a moral standard undermines religious/moral authority, but it does not explicitly claim homosexual people threaten religion or culture in a direct way.
itt_response.anxiety_discomfort: No strong fear or unease is generated about homosexual people. The tone is explanatory/debunking (“where do we get the idea…?”) and the imagery is a studio presenter plus animal footage used to argue against using animal behavior as a moral standard. Discomfort is directed toward the argument’s logic, not toward the group itself.
itt_response.stereotype_support: The video does not reinforce common negative stereotypes about homosexual people (e.g., violence, criminality, disease). However, it does invoke a moral comparison strategy: it references animals engaging in behaviors like infanticide and theft to argue that “natural” does not equal “okay,” which is a rhetorical move that can indirectly stigmatize the underlying pro-LGBT argument rather than the group’s traits.
itt_response.hostility_prescription: No. The video does not endorse exclusion, harassment, or harm toward homosexual people. It argues for a Christian moral position and rejects using animal behavior as a standard, without prescribing hostility.

The emperor was a big fan of literature.As well as music, he ordered a census to find out the beauty of his empire's women, dissatisfied with the 3000 women in his palace. These women living in ancient China to ancient Rome, faced countless trials and tribulations. Their lives were far from ideal, marred by societal stigma, rivalries and uncertainty.However, they found opportunities within their struggles, achieving social elevation, protection, and even experiencing motherhood.🎼Their experiences provide us with a unique viewpoint of historical power dynamics, societal norms, and gender relations.Their stories make us ponder about current gender roles and relationships. Title: I’m so thankful to have had such an understanding supervisor! 💗 #autism #autistic #worklife #adhd
Transcript: I know I look like I'd be really nice, but I was actually a menace when I first started working full time. Turns out your coworkers don't appreciate commentary on how they could get better at their jobs. It's not my fault that other people's mistakes are just more intellectually stimulating to talk about. Besides, if I asked about their weekend plans, they tell me. And who has time for all that. I don't. So probably tired of my nonsense and maybe tired of getting complaints. My supervisor made me a deal. I could text her all of my rude thoughts as long as I didn't.😊Say them out loud In return, she promised to give some sort of response, even if it was just like a string of empathetic emojis. few months of keeping this arrangement resulted in two things.1 increased self-awareness, slowing down to type your thoughts because you might get in trouble for saying the wrong ones out loud really forces you to think before you speak and two, improvement in my workplace relationships being more thoughtful about what I let come out of my mouth gave me space to have more conventional interactions, even if it was under the threat of punishment.
Label: Normal

itt_response.target_salience: The video’s title and implied message center on a woman described as a “nasty harlot,” with the woman depicted as the central subject of “cleaning the streets.” No other protected group (e.g., race, religion, nationality) is clearly targeted; the salient group is a sexualized/“immoral” woman (gendered moral outgroup).
itt_response.realistic_threat: The frames show street/urban scenes and a woman being confronted/handled, but there is no clear evidence of a material threat like violence, weapon use, or direct harm to jobs/safety/resources by a specific protected group. The threat framing is more about removal/discipline than demonstrated real-world harm.
itt_response.symbolic_threat: Yes. The title explicitly frames the target as morally contaminating (“nasty harlot”) and positions “cleaning the streets” as restoring social order. This is a symbolic threat to morals/values and perceived community standards, with the target treated as an embodiment of “nasty” immorality.
itt_response.anxiety_discomfort: The video uses confrontational street imagery, aggressive/hostile body language, and rapid/blurred editing that can heighten discomfort. The overall tone (implied by the title) suggests moral panic and justification for removal, which can produce unease about the target being present in public spaces.
itt_response.stereotype_support: It reinforces a negative stereotype that a woman labeled as a “harlot” is inherently “nasty,” socially harmful, and deserving of expulsion/discipline. The “evidence” is not factual behavior shown in detail, but the label and the depiction of the woman as the problem to be removed.
itt_response.hostility_prescription: The accumulated threat perception (moral contamination + “cleaning the streets” removal) supports exclusion and potentially harm/abuse as a corrective action. The framing implies endorsement of hostility toward the targeted woman (removal/disciplining), even though explicit physical violence is not clearly shown in the provided frames.

Title: China is a "Mistress Nation"
Transcript: 。I can't believe this is the country we're scared of going to war with, look at their top officials and businessmen they're all just getting mistresses, so this guy in the pink shirt is one of the executives of Perol China and he's seen here parading in the city of Chengdu with his mistress and after this incident happened it went viral in China the guy got fired, his wife comes out and claims that the girl he's holding hands with is actually his goddaughter.And soon, this prompted a bunch of cosplays of the couple. Some of these are pretty uncanny. Her dress has now become a fashion trend in China.And another one of the executives, I believe this is for railroad China, the third division or third Bureau, another top executive seen here parading the same streets in the same city, getting caught on camera, cheating with what likely is another mistress. so don't be scared of what China is really like, It corruption, its mistressses.
Label: Hateful

itt_response.target_salience: The video centers on Chinese people/China as a social group, with particular focus on Chinese “top officials and businessmen” depicted as engaging in extramarital affairs (“mistresses”). China is made central to the message via repeated on-screen text (“THIS IS THE REAL CHINA… MISTRESSES”) and narrative claims about national character.
itt_response.realistic_threat: Yes, but indirectly and with weak/unsupported evidence. It implies a threat through corruption and moral misconduct by “executives” (e.g., claims of being fired, cheating, and corruption). However, the depicted acts are personal/sexual infidelity rather than clear threats to jobs, safety, health, or territory; the “threat” is asserted rather than demonstrated with concrete impacts.
itt_response.symbolic_threat: Yes. The video frames China as morally degraded (“corruption, its mistressses”) and uses sexual infidelity as a proxy for cultural/moral decay. It also uses war-related language (“country we’re scared of going to war with”) to suggest that China’s alleged moral corruption is part of why it is dangerous.
itt_response.anxiety_discomfort: Yes. It creates unease by using sensational framing (viral scandal narrative), accusatory tone (“I can’t believe…”, “so don’t be scared…”), and repeated captions emphasizing “mistresses.” Imagery shows couples walking in public while captions label them as cheating, and the editing/narration links these scenes to national danger and corruption.
itt_response.stereotype_support: It reinforces a negative stereotype that Chinese leadership/business elites are uniformly corrupt and sexually immoral, presented as representative of “the real China.” The video treats specific alleged scandals (or reenactments/claims) as evidence of a broader national character, using the “mistress” trope to generalize wrongdoing to an entire group.
itt_response.hostility_prescription: The video does not explicitly call for violence, but it does endorse hostility/exclusion in a soft form by urging viewers not to be “scared” of China while simultaneously portraying China as corrupt and morally untrustworthy. This can function as justification for distrust, dehumanization, or support for punitive attitudes toward Chinese people/nation.

The doctor. bless your heart.Now, don't test me, Mr. Smith, I am a Christian and a nurse, which means I can save your life twice. When I said last week that I needed more patience I.I think you misheard me, and you gave me more patience. Quick complaining. you're getting paid to do nothing. I see what you did here.

itt_response.target_salience: The video centers on Christians (religious identity) and nurses (occupation). The Christian identity is used as a key explanation for the speaker’s authority and behavior, while “nurse” is part of the claimed role-based competence. The message repeatedly foregrounds “I am a Christian and a nurse,” making Christians highly salient to the narrative.
itt_response.realistic_threat: No. The video does not depict Christians as causing material harm to jobs, safety, health, or resources. The setting is a hospital/clinical environment and the speaker’s actions are framed as caregiving/authority rather than as a threat. Any conflict is interpersonal (e.g., “Mr. Smith”) and not about Christians harming others.
itt_response.symbolic_threat: Yes, in a limited/indirect way. The transcript includes religiously framed moral language (“forgive me for I have seen more genitals in a harlot,” “I am a Christian and a nurse”) that ties Christian identity to moral judgment and correction. However, it does not present Christians as a threat to others’ values; rather, it uses Christian moral framing to police behavior. This is more moralizing than intergroup threat.
itt_response.anxiety_discomfort: No strong threat-based anxiety is created. The tone appears comedic (“POV… #funny #nursing”), with a nurse speaking in a dramatic/performative manner. Editing shows quick cuts and subtitles, but the emotional effect is more humorous/over-the-top than fear-inducing. Discomfort may come from sexual/moral language, but not from fear of Christians as a group.
itt_response.stereotype_support: The video invokes a stereotype of Christians as morally judgmental and as using religious authority to reprimand others (e.g., confession-like phrasing and “Christian” as justification for control/competence). It also includes sexualized/moralized language (“genitals,” “harlot”) as evidence for the speaker’s stance. These are negative portrayals of religious moralism, but they are not a direct claim that Christians are inherently dangerous.
itt_response.hostility_prescription: No. The accumulated framing does not endorse exclusion, violence, or harm toward Christians. The speaker asserts authority over a specific person (“Mr. Smith”) in a comedic, confrontational manner, but there is no call to target Christians as a group.

Title: I want a HOMOSEXUAL Cake🍰! Jesse Lee Peterson Interview
Transcript: My wife and I, who just say, I want a really good cake. I like dessert, and I heard from my friends that this place has good cake. And so you go to that place and you don't expect to be discriminated against. You don't expect to be vi. agree with that. Will you go in there and you say, I want a.。A gay cake, a homosexual cake What is homosexual cake because I think the if you and I eat the same cake, my cake isn't isn't homosexual because I'm gay just like if we order pizza and you take a slice and I take a slice, it's not a gay pizza but why would you pass up a gay bake bakery to go to a Christian bakery that you know just not want to service you. I don't want to presuppose that.I know no, you know that most Christians don't want to participate in that I don't think that's why they I mean, you have Psal that do, but most I don't think that's true because you went to a Christian.
Label: Offensive

itt_response.target_salience: The video centers on gay/lesbian people (sexual orientation) as the subject of a hypothetical scenario about ordering a “gay/homosexual cake.” This group is central to the message because the discussion repeatedly contrasts service for gay customers versus service for “Christian” customers.
itt_response.realistic_threat: No clear material threat is depicted. The transcript focuses on discrimination in service (refusal to provide a product/service) rather than threats to physical safety, health, or resources. There is no evidence of violence, coercion, or direct harm beyond exclusion from a bakery.
itt_response.symbolic_threat: Yes. The video frames the issue as tied to religious identity and moral/religious boundaries: it contrasts a “gay/homosexual cake” with a “Christian bakery,” implying that Christian beliefs determine whether service is provided. This links sexual orientation to perceived incompatibility with religious values.
itt_response.anxiety_discomfort: The video creates discomfort primarily through the theme of anticipated discrimination and refusal of service. It uses edited on-screen captions emphasizing “homosexual/gay cake,” “discriminated against,” and “don’t want to service you,” which heightens the sense that gay people may be unwelcome. No threatening imagery or music cues are evident from the frames provided.
itt_response.stereotype_support: It invokes a stereotype that “most Christians” (or Christian businesses) do not want to participate in serving gay customers, presenting this as a likely or typical behavior. It also implies that gay identity is the defining factor that triggers refusal, reinforcing an association between Christianity and anti-gay exclusion.
itt_response.hostility_prescription: The accumulated threat perception does not clearly prescribe violence or direct harm. However, it does support an exclusionary framing in practice by centering the idea that gay customers should expect discrimination and by positioning religious refusal as a key conflict point. The overall implication leans toward endorsing separation/denial of service based on sexual orientation rather than mutual inclusion.

If you're the person everyone gossips to, it's because you invite that shit into your social circle. Nobody fucking gossips to me. And I don't just mean in this business just in life. Nobody do you have the fucking balls to gossip to me because they know exactly what they're gonna get if they do it. what I just said, hey, you seem very concerned about this person. Let's go talk to them about it. Like I don't think that's necessary.I loathe the gossip if it isn't clear. it's one of the number one killers in business and teams. If you're genuinely concerned about the person, go fucking talk to them. Don't make assumptions and go gossip about them to other people.

itt_response.realistic_threat: No. The video does not present gossipers as posing material threats to safety, health, resources, or territory. The only “harm” referenced is reputational/professional impact (e.g., “kills me,” “number one killers in business”), which is framed as social/professional damage rather than a concrete physical or resource threat.
itt_response.symbolic_threat: Weak/indirect. Editing uses on-screen captions emphasizing certainty and blame (“it’s because you invite that shit,” “they know exactly what they’re gonna get”), which can create discomfort about being the target of gossip, but not fear of a group’s existence or violence.
itt_response.stereotype_support: None clearly tied to protected characteristics. The video reinforces a general negative stereotype about gossipers as untrustworthy/assumption-making and about the “gossip target” as inviting it through social behavior (“you invite that shit into your social circle”). Evidence is asserted through personal experience and rhetorical certainty rather than factual substantiation.
itt_response.hostility_prescription: Low. The accumulated perception leads to discouraging gossip and prescribing direct confrontation/communication (“go talk to them,” “don’t make assumptions”). While it uses aggressive language, it does not endorse exclusion, violence, or harm toward a protected group.

They are central to the joke/complaint framing ("biggest attention whore," "influencers," "train wreck," etc.).

The only explicit harm is interpersonal profanity (e.g., "Fuck you, Debbie"), but it is not tied to a broader claim of physical danger or resource/territorial threat.
itt_response.symbolic_threat: Yes, in a symbolic/moral sense. Discomfort is created through a mocking, accusatory tone and repeated negative labeling ("attention whore," "fuck you," "tired of them," "bullshit," "foul"), plus rapid montage of many yellow block figures in a game environment. The imagery itself is non-threatening, but the text overlays and commentary generate irritation/annoyance rather than fear.
itt_response.stereotype_support: Negative stereotypes about influencers/celebrity culture are reinforced: that they are attention-seeking, dishonest/exaggerating ("far fetched stories"), and collectively shallow or manipulative ("hive mind of bullshit," "majority of Instagram influencers"). These are presented as justification for the negative judgments via popularity/visibility and perceived behavior.
itt_response.hostility_prescription: No clear endorsement of violence or exclusion is shown. However, the accumulated framing supports hostility in the form of contempt and dehumanizing dismissal (e.g., calling them "attention whore," "train wreck," "bullshit," and wishing harm in a non-specific way like "fuck you"; one line suggests arrest for a specific person). Overall, it trends toward verbal hostility rather than explicit calls for harm against a protected class.

Title: ‘Sorry love, you’re not a celebrity, you’re just gay.’ Douglas Murray on Pride #pride #lgbt
Transcript: I've objected it most of my life to the whole pride thing, mainly on taste grounds, it has to be said, I don't begrudge anyone having a party, I do begrudge people mistaking their sexuality for some massively important signifier which demands that other people then not just respect them but pay them some special dues and actually in actual fact you could see this so I've stumbled across pride a couple of times in my time I've never gone but I've stumbled across it a few times and one of the things that on a simple.of aesthetics, a taste that I deeply dislike.I seeing a bunch of disco gaze on top of a bus waving to the public as if they're celebrities, sorry love, you're not a celebrity, just gay, that's all and you shouldn't treat the general public as your audience waiting for them to celebrate the magnificent wonderful you so I think there's just this nasty narcissism at the heart of this and I've got no time for it.😡
Label: Hateful

itt_response.target_salience: The video centers on LGBTQ people—specifically gay men and Pride participants—as the subject of the speaker’s criticism. Their presence is central to the message (Pride parade imagery and repeated references to “gay” and “disco gays”).
itt_response.realistic_threat: No material/physical threat is presented. The speaker does not claim LGBTQ people threaten safety, jobs, or resources; the argument is framed as personal dislike and social/behavioral entitlement rather than harm to material conditions.
itt_response.symbolic_threat: Yes. The speaker frames LGBTQ Pride as a threat to social norms and values—arguing that Pride participants “mistak[e] their sexuality” as an important signifier that demands special treatment (“pay them some special dues”) and that the public should not be treated as their audience. This is a symbolic threat to cultural expectations about how sexuality should be expressed and how others should respond.
itt_response.anxiety_discomfort: The video creates discomfort/hostility through a mocking, dismissive tone (“sorry love, you’re not a celebrity, just gay”) and by portraying Pride participants as self-important (“nasty narcissism”). Editing and on-screen captions emphasize derogatory framing (e.g., “disco gays on top of a bus,” “waving… as if they’re celebrities”).
itt_response.stereotype_support: It reinforces negative stereotypes that LGBTQ people (especially gay men) are narcissistic, attention-seeking, and entitled to special recognition. The “disco” framing and “celebrity” comparison are used as purported evidence for these character judgments.
itt_response.hostility_prescription: The accumulated perception supports exclusionary social treatment rather than direct violence: it prescribes that LGBTQ Pride participants should not receive deference or special status from the general public (“you shouldn’t treat the general public as your audience”). This is an implicit endorsement of social marginalization/denial of legitimacy.

Title: Heart Shape Swiss Roll Cake🩷🍰 #cake #shorts
Transcript: Today, I made a heartshaped Swiss roll cake and never made this before and just crossed my fingers that it would turn out. I started by making the pattern mixture and kept it super simple with pink hearts. while that was chilling in the fridge, I mixed together the cake batter and I added pink food color into that as well. After that, I had to whip up a moringgue. and I messed it up on the first try, but made it correctly the second time. So that's what count. I gently folded that into the cake batter and poured it onto my pattern. I smoothed it out, popped it in the oven. And just like that, it baked. and I had to roll it up.Right away, so at one crack, as you can see, I was very excited it came out in one piece while that's completely cooling. I cut up my raspberries and made my whip cream filling. and this is my strong suit working with frosting. so I was very excited to fill this cake and put all the raspberries in. Then I had to reroll it into the official heart shape and let it chill in the fridge for four hours, which was not fun because I just wanted to eat it right away, but I was definitely worth the weight. It ended up looking orangey on camera, but I swear it's pink. I had the best time making it and an even better time eating it.😊
Label: Normal

Title: Who Made The Pride Flag? Queer History 101 #shorts
Transcript: 🎼Welcome to Queer History 101 Part 3. We all know the Rainbow flag as the symbol of the LGBTQ+ community. But why the Rainbow flag was created in 1978 by artist, veteran and drag performer Gilbert Baker He was commissioned to create a flag with San Diego's pride paradeade by another gay icon politician Harvey Mil, The different colorss within the flag are intended to represent togetherness Since LGBTQ people come in all races, ages and genders and Rabows are both natural and beautiful. The original flag featured8 colors.Each having a different meaning, hot pink for sex, red for life, orange for healing, yellow for sunlight, green for nature, herpoise for art, indigo for harmony violet for spirit, Sun flags added black and brown stripes to honor black and brown members of the community. A recent update is the progress flag which featureses the Chevron to the side of the original flag to honor trans as well as black and brown community members.😊
Label: Normal

Title: I read the shortest book ever written and here are my thoughts 💭📚🫣 #booktok #booktube #bookreview
Transcript: 🎼I decided I wanted to read the shortest book ever written. So I looked up a few options on Google. There were a ton of books to choose from, but I noticed that one in particular kept coming up over and over again, not just because it was 25 pages, but also because it was apparently really, really good. I was a little on the fence about buying it because the topic was so unusual, But because of how many good reviews it had. And because of the length. I just decided to give it a try. I finished the book in one sitting. And I got to say it's definitely worth the hyp. That book was the yellow wallpaper.

Title: One Minute With Nature's Scariest Eagle: Harpy Eagle
Transcript: The Hary Eagleagle is one of the most powerful and largest rappttors globally this bird is found in the sparsely inhabited rainforests of Central and South America.It is perhaps one of the most intimidating birds in the world with its retractable feather crown and razor sharp talons that are about four to five inches, just the same as a grizzly bear's claw.Those talons give the hary enough power and grip to lift prey up to their own weight This eagle is a silent predator that glides across the forest like a stealth drone despite its wingspan reaching up to6 and a half feet.Harpies feed on sloths, oposssums and monkeys, instead of migrating, they stay in one place and establish their territory.Monogamous and with a lifespan of 35 to 45 years, a bonded pair may stay together for 25 to 30 years.

Title: Women Need To Stop Dressing Like Sluts...
Transcript: Every single person needs to be accountable for their behavior, their actions, how they dress, if you're looking for that, if you're looking for that one night stand and you're dressed like you want that one night stand and you're putting that out there, do what you want, your're a grown adult, go right ahead.Yeah.If you're not looking for that and you're dressed like you're looking for that and somebody assumes by nature of what you're looking like, that you're looking for that, don't be mad about it. be rational about it, Think about it, say, well, what message am I putting out there, You don't like the message you're putting out there changing, you can still be sexy. There's a difference. So what I'm saying is, yeah, society can be, you know sometimes with this messaging confusing this.But it's not just men that have to be accountable for their actions, it's women too, we all need to be accountable, we all need to take ownership of what we're bringing to the table and what message we're putting out there.😡
Label: Offensive

itt_response.target_salience: Women (specifically women’s sexualized appearance/dressing choices) are central to the message. The transcript repeatedly addresses women’s accountability for how they dress and the “message” it sends, while also referencing men’s assumptions.
itt_response.realistic_threat: No. The video does not present women as a material threat to safety, jobs, health, or territory. It discusses interpersonal sexual expectations/assumptions rather than concrete harm or resource/territory conflict.
itt_response.symbolic_threat: Yes, in a moral/values sense. The video frames women’s clothing choices as communicating sexual availability and thus implicating social norms/morality (“what message am I putting out there,” “society… messaging confusing,” “there’s a difference” between being sexy and dressing like a “slut”). This treats sexualized presentation as a threat to social/sexual norms and expectations.
itt_response.anxiety_discomfort: Some. The tone is admonishing and corrective (“stop,” “be accountable,” “don’t be mad,” “think about it”), which can create discomfort by implying that women’s appearance will predict others’ sexual interpretations and that women should manage that risk. Imagery shows women speaking on camera with emphatic gestures and on-screen captions reinforcing accountability and messaging; no fear-based visuals or music cues are evident from the frames provided.
itt_response.stereotype_support: Yes. It reinforces a stereotype that women’s clothing/appearance is a reliable indicator of sexual intent (e.g., dressing to attract a one-night stand) and that others’ assumptions are “by nature” of what they look like. It also uses slut-shaming framing (“dressing like sluts”) and contrasts “sexy” vs. “wrong” dressing as evidence of moral/sexual character.
itt_response.hostility_prescription: No direct endorsement of violence or exclusion is present. However, it implicitly prescribes behavioral control/blame toward women (accountability for dressing to prevent being misread/assumed), which can function as a mild hostility/disciplinary stance rather than overt harm.

Title: The Life and Death of Diana Dors
Transcript: ？🎼Diana Doris was born on October 23,1931 in Swindon, Wiltshire, England. Her birth name was Diana Mary Fuck. I can see why studios wanted her to change her last name. Shes known for being an actress, model, author and singer. She hated being called the British Marin Monroe. But let's face it. She was the British Marin Monroe. She was a blonde bombshell who kept the tabloids busy. She won a beauty contest at age 13 and started acting. She appeared older than she was. Her roles slowly got better. She started many British film.TV shows and plays before moving to Hollywood. She'd go back to England and appear in movies and come back to Hollywood a lot. Her second husband was Richard Dawson, who would later host the game show called the Family Feud. She always remained captivating even in her older years. She recorded a solo album and made a few songs for soundtracks. She was married three times and had three children. She died in 1984 from ovarian cancer. Her last husband named Alllan Lake, so distraught that he committed suicide five months after she died. She was 52 years old.
Label: Normal

Title: What are harpies ? #harpy  #grimoireofmyths #fantasy
Transcript: Yeah.In Greek mythology, the harpies were known as Lasser goddesses and they were personifications of violent winds, it was often believed that the harpies were responsible for the abrupt disappearances of some people however, the harpies became more well known with their presence in the story of the Argonauts in which the group of heroes stumbled into the harpies while they were tormenting King Phineas in the earlier version, the harpies were wind spirits that kidnapped people and made things vanish according to this,The Harrpies were known as the Harrpies, Once they kidnapped the daughters of the king of Myitus, Panderos, and took them to provide services to a group of female spirits, known as the Fuies.These spirits were very powerful, it is commonly believed that the harpies are the offspring of the ancient sea God thhomamus and the Oceanic Electra, if this is the case, then the harpies would be sisters to the goddess iris, who is known for her role as a messenger.

Title: "Trans Woman" Kicked Out Of Bathroom And Loses It
Transcript: 🎼I had a neighbor confront me for using the woman's restroom Everyone wants to know that I'm a trans woman. I've always been known as Kaylee. I've always used she her pronoun and I'm like is there a problem, She's like, yeah, you're using in the woman's restroom, you're a man and I said I'm a trans woman she's like no you're a man and honestly I just lost it It just lost it and just start screaming and swear I just couldn't handle it everyone should know I'm a trans woman with the beard and all.With the hairline all the way in the back, like Rosa Parks on the bus.
Label: Offensive

itt_response.hostility_prescription: The video does not explicitly endorse violence or harm. However, the neighbor’s stance (denying restroom access based on being “a man”) reflects an exclusionary prescription. The overall threat perception centers on restricting access to women’s spaces, which aligns with hostility/exclusion rather than acceptance.

Title: How to come out as queer to Christian family
Transcript: I was fortunate enough that they still supported me but regardless of that support or not, I was not going to wait on their acceptance or approval to start living the life that I truly believe that I'm supposed to be living, but with that it also gave me this understanding of like grace.うん。Because it took me 12 years to get to where I am today right and that's me actively reconstructing my faith and actively asking God the hard questions and so to assume or have the expectation for somebody who has believed this their entire life to all of a sudden just throw that out the window I feel like it's just kind of an unrealistic expectation so for me I develop patience and grace and understanding that they may not,You know I'm saying to ever come to the same side that I am on as far as how I believe and my sexuality and my spirituality can be together.
Label: Normal

itt_response.realistic_threat: No. The video does not depict queer people or Christians as posing material threats to safety, jobs, health, or resources. The speaker describes personal experience and boundaries around living authentically, not harm or coercion by either group.
itt_response.symbolic_threat: Low to none. While the speaker contrasts sexuality/spirituality with Christian family expectations, the framing is not that Christians are a threat to values or that queer identity is inherently dangerous. Instead, the speaker emphasizes grace, patience, and understanding that others may not reach the same beliefs.
itt_response.anxiety_discomfort: Minimal. The tone appears reflective and explanatory (talking about patience, grace, and reconstructing faith). There is no visible imagery or editing suggesting fear, danger, or intimidation; the discomfort is interpersonal/emotional (uncertainty about acceptance) rather than fear of a group.
itt_response.stereotype_support: None clearly. The video does not present Christians as inherently rejecting, hateful, or irrational, nor does it present queer people as inherently immoral or harmful. It explicitly acknowledges support may exist and argues against unrealistic expectations of immediate acceptance.
itt_response.hostility_prescription: No. The accumulated perception does not endorse exclusion, hostility, or harm. The speaker advocates patience, grace, and continued authentic living without seeking approval.

Title: Random woman holds child, but the truth behind picture makes people cry #fact
Transcript: 🎼This is a random woman holding a child on a plane, but the truth behind this photo made people cry. A Texas mother called Becca Kinsey was traveling alone with her two young sons, but little did she know what was in store for her. You see her sons were exhausted and they kept screaming. the entire time it seemed nothing was going to plan and even getting through security was a struggle. Becca was so close to breaking down when suddenly a stranger approached her and said something she couldn't believe the kind stranger asked her if.She needed help, and along with two other women, helped Becca get through security, as well as with her bags. And after her son screamed during take off, an unnamed woman held Becca's son who would immediately relax and settle, Becca was so grateful for all of the random acts of kindness by people who owed her nothing. She took this photo to show the world how much she appreciated it. and to also remind herself to never travel without her husband again..

itt_response.target_salience: The video centers on a person associated with prostitution (“someone that was into prostitution business”) and treats that category as the relevant social group. This group is central to the message because the entire claim is about whether money obtained through prostitution can be accepted by the church.

Editing uses bold captions and repeated emphasis on “value” and “demonic altar,” plus dramatic delivery/music cues, to create discomfort about accepting such money in a religious setting.
itt_response.stereotype_support: The video reinforces a negative moral/spiritual stereotype: that people involved in prostitution (and money derived from it) are inherently linked to demonic forces and are unfit for legitimate religious purposes. It treats this as evidence through a theological assertion rather than through factual support.
itt_response.hostility_prescription: Indirectly. While it does not call for violence or direct harm, it prescribes exclusion from religious acceptance (the offering/money should not be received by the Lord/righteous altar). This functions as a form of hostility/exclusion toward the prostitution-associated category in the context of worship and legitimacy.

Title: How to not hurt your lower back on deadlift
Transcript: 🎼.One of the most crucial factors to the deadlift is your initial setup it will make or break the exercise and pretty much determine long term if you're gonna get injured from the deadlift or not two main things you want to consider when you're setting up for deadlift first off, it's a distance between the bar and your shins you want this to be ideally as close to your shins as possible when you pull up just before you even begin, you want the bar to be close to your shins if not just right directly through your midfoot, and the second factor is why I call pulling the slack out of the barn, which looks and sounds like this.You hear that, notice how the bar.Kind of makes a little bit of noise. That is slack being pulled out the bar. If you don't pull the slack out of the bar, you're going to be kind jerking the weight up and it makes it a lot easier for you to lose your brace. So instead you come down, have the bar close to your foot, take a deep breath.Pull slack out, that.

Title: The Harpy Eagle
Transcript: 🎼The Hary Eagleagle is one of the largest and most powerful eagles in the world, with a wingspan of up to 7 feet and a weight of up to 20. They are found in Central and South America, and their range includes the Amazon rainforest.The Harry Eagle is a top predator in its ecosystem and is known for its incredible hunting skills.🎼They are capable of taking down prey as large as monkeys, sloths and even deer. They are named after the haries of Greek mythology, which were mythical creatures with the head of a woman and the body of a bird.Hary Eagleagles have large and powerful talents that can be up to5 inches long, which they use to catch and kill their prey.🎼They have a distinctive crest of feathers on their head that they can raise and lower communicate with other eagles. Hary eagles are considered a threatened species due to habitat loss and hunting.While they are generally not a threat to humans, Hary Eagleagles have been known to attack people who encroach on their territory or threaten their young.

Title: Discover the Harpy Eagle: A Mighty Predator of the Amazon
Transcript: 🎼Deep in the heart of the Amazon rainforest lives a creature that inspires awe and fear in equal measure. Meet the Harrpy eagle, one of the largest and most powerful birds of prey in the world.With a wingspan of up to7 feet and a weight of up to 20 pounds, the Harrpy eagle is a formidable predator that rules the canopy of the forest.🎼Its talons are among the largest and strongest of any bird, capable of crushing bones and tearing flesh with ease. The Harrpy Eagleles's diet consists mainly of sloths, monkeys, and other mammals that inhabit the treetops.Its hunting strategy is to ambush its prey from above, using its powerful wings and sharp eyesight to surprise and overwhelm them.🎼Despite its size and power, the Harrpy eagle is a rare and elusive creature that is seldom seen by humans, but its presence in the forest is a sign of a healthy ecosystem where predators and prey coexist in a delicate balance.

Title: Minor Decoy Program
Transcript: Have you ever been annoyed by a server or bartender asking for your ID Well here's why they do it Okay I was a server for 23 years most of it in California and in California they have the alcoholic beverage control This department has something called the minor decoy program where they get people that are under age 20 and the undercover cop will go in with this minor decoy and order an alcoholic drink from a bartender or server in the US you have to be 21 years old right so the under 20 person order and if you are the server,Or the bartender and you don't make sure they have a legal ID before serving them the drink You're busted This is what happens The undercover cop will then make you reenact handing the drink to the minor decoy and they take a photo of it for everybody to see and then you are fine It's misdemeanor you're fine Also the bar or restaurant gets fine So don't be offended particularly in California and if this happens in another states let me know but the server or bartender is probably scared that they're gonna get fined or whatever so That's it.

The country hadn't actually made any progress.
